A Deontic View of the Intentionality of Desire," references the work of Austrian philosopher Alexius Meinong, particularly his theories on "objectives" and "oughts" (Sollen). Meinong's significant works in this area include "On Emotional Presentation" (1917) and "Investigations in Theory of Objects and Psychology" (1904), as well as other writings on value theory from 1912, 1921, and 1923.[1](https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/meinong/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alexius_Meinong), [3](https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/fall2012/entries/meinong/), [4](https://open.nupress.northwestern.edu/books/emotional-presentation/), [5](https://openresearchlibrary.org/content/91af4565-41c7-46a9-97c4-5aad1421ae79), [6](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/345523064_Meinong's_Theory_of_Meaning), [7](https://www.ontology.co/biblio/meinonga-editions.htm), [8](https://usmai-umcp.alma.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/fulldisplay?docid=alma990001782990108238&context=L&vid=01USMAI_UMCP:UMCP&lang=en&adaptor=Local%20Search%20Engine&tab=Everything&query=sub%2Cexact%2CSocial%20conflict%2CAND&mode=advanced&offset=30), [9](https://catalog.library.vanderbilt.edu/discovery/fulldisplay?vid=01VAN_INST%3Avanui&tab=Everything&docid=alma991019234519703276&lang=en&context=L), [10](https://www.ontology.co/meinonga.htm), [11](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonexistent_objects)
